elements.sent-2411-001: Learning high-dimensional distributions is a significant challenge in machine learning and statistics in this era of big data.
elements.sent-2411-002: Classical research has mostly concentrated on asymptotic analysis of such data when the underlying distribution exhibits structured patterns like product distributions, tree structures, or Bayesian networks.
elements.sent-2411-003: While existing works [Bhattacharyya et al.: SICOMP 2023, Daskalakis et al.: STOC 2021, Choo et al.: ALT 2024] focus on discrete distributions, the current work addresses the tree structure learning problem for Gaussian distributions, providing efficient algorithms with solid theoretical guarantees.

elements.sent-2411-014: Learning high-dimensional distributions has been one of the cornerstone problems in the field of machine learning and statistics.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this era of big data, data is being generated in massive amounts, and traditional methods of analyzing data are often not feasible.
elements.sent-2411-016: As a result, over the past two decades, there has been significant interest in developing efficient algorithmic techniques to analyze high dimensional data more efficiently, which also have sound theoretical guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-017: Although learning an arbitrary high dimensional distribution is hard in general, often these tasks become easier when these distributions have some structure.
elements.sent-2411-018: Examples include when these unknown distributions over $n$-dimensional Hamming cube is a product distribution, tree-structured distribution, or bounded degree Bayesian networks.
elements.sent-2411-019: Probabilistic graphical models (see \cite{koller2009probabilistic ) were proposed to address the above situation of succinctly modeling high-dimensional probability distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Concrete examples of such models include the class of Ising models, Bayesian networks, and Gaussian graphical models.
elements.sent-2411-021: The focus of this work is the Gaussian graphical model.
elements.sent-2411-022: Since its introduction, it has been used to model important datasets across various scientific disciplines such as modeling brain connectivity networks: \cite{huang2010learning,varoquaux2010brain , gene regulatory networks: \cite{basso2005reverse,menendez2010gene,schafer2005learning,wille2004sparse etc. A Gaussian graphical model (GGM) is simply a multivariate Gaussian distribution $X\sim N(\mu,\Sigma)$, with the mean vector $\mu$ and the covariance matrix $\Sigma$, over the sample space $\mathbb{R ^n$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Its probability density is given by: \begin{equation}\pr[X=x]=\frac{1}{\paren{2\pi\det(\Sigma)}^{\frac{n}{2}}} \exp\paren{ -\paren{x-\mu}\Sigma^{-1}\paren{x-\mu}^\top }\end{equation} We can think of the random variable $X$ to be consisting of $n$ (possibly correlated) component random variables $X=(X_1,\dots, X_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-024: The inverse of the covariance matrix $\Theta=\Sigma^{-1}$, called the precision matrix, encodes the conditional independence relations of the component random variables as follows.
elements.sent-2411-025: First, construct a dependency graph $G_{\Theta}$ on the $n$ vertices: $[n]$, whose $(i,j)$-th entry of the adjacency matrix $A$ is $1$ iff $\Theta_{i,j}\neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Let $\mathrm{nbr (i)$ be the set of nodes adjacent to $i$ in $G_{\Theta}$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Then, any particular component $X_i$, conditioned on the set of neighboring components $ X_{\mathrm{nbr (i) :=\{X_j:j\in \mathrm{nbr (i)\}$ is independent of all other components: \begin{equation}X_i \bot X_{nbr(i)} \mid X\setminus \paren{\{X_i\}\cup X_{nbr(i)}}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Specifically, in this paper, we consider the set of tree-structured Gaussian graphical models where the graph $G_{\Theta}$ is tree-structured, namely it does not have any cycles.
elements.sent-2411-029: Perhaps, this is the simplest class of GGMs one can think of apart from the class of product distributions where $\Sigma$ is diagonal and the graph $G_{\Theta}$ does not have any edges.
elements.sent-2411-030: Tree-structured graphical models are used in Biology to model phylogenetic networks~\citep[Page 53]{jones2021bayesian , \cite{boix2022chow and references therein.}}

elements.sent-2411-032: A tree $\widehat{T}$ is said to be $\eps$-approximate tree for $P$ if $\kl(P||P_{\widehat{T ) \leq \kl(P||P^*) + \eps$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: A tree-structured distribution is said to be $T$-structured if the underlying tree is $T$.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the structure learning problem, we are interested in approximately recovering the best tree structure for the unknown distribution $P$ from random samples.
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, a natural approach to estimating the best tree structure is to estimate the pairwise mutual informations and simply return the estimated maximum spanning tree.
elements.sent-2411-036: This algorithm, which we refer to as the Chow-Liu algorithm, has been extensively studied from asymptotic viewpoints where the goal is to accurately recover the underlying tree structure as the number of samples tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-037: In \cite{tan2010learningexp , the authors studied the ``error exponent'' for the maximum-likelihood estimator of the tree structure, \cite{tan2010error studied the error exponents for composite hypothesis testing of Markov forest distributions, \cite{tan2010learninghypothesis considered learning two tree graphical models to classify future observations into one of two categories, \cite{tan2010scaling studied the problem of learning forests for discrete graphical models by removing edges from the Chow–Liu tree.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: See the PhD thesis of \citep[Chapter 7]{jones2021bayesian for several related discussions and references.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In contrast, a series of recent papers have looked at the question of prediction-centric learning, where the goal is not to recover the exact tree structure but to only {\em approximately recover the underlying tree structure such that the best distribution on the recovered tree structure is close to the true one in some distance measure such as KL divergence or variation distance, see \cite{bresler2020learning, boix2022chow, bhattacharyya2023near, DBLP:conf/stoc/DaskalakisP21,DBLP:journals/corr/abs-2310-06333 and the references therein.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The idea is that such an approximate structure should be good enough for downstream inference tasks.
elements.sent-2411-041: Despite the simplicity and usefulness of tree-structured GGMs, a finite sample guarantee of approximately learning these models from samples has remained elusive so far.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our current paper initiates the study of prediction-centric learning of Gaussian tree models in KL divergence and settles down its sample complexity using efficient algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-043: Specifically, we characterize the minimum number of samples needed to learn an $\eps$-approximate tree in both realizable and non-realizable cases, up to small factors by giving almost tight sample-complexity upper and lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-044: We show that the Chow-Liu algorithm always takes a near-optimal number of samples in terms of the approximation error ($\eps$) and the number of component random variables ($n$), to achieve a desired approximation guarantee for structure learning in both the realizable and non-realizable cases.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Empirical-CMI$(m)$}\label{alg:condmitester} Get $m$ samples $\bar{X}=(X_1,\dots,X_m), \bar{Y}=(Y_1,\dots,Y_m), \bar{Z}=(Z_1,\dots,Z_m)$. \Comment{Unconditional MI tester} $\wh{\rho}_{X,Z}\gets\frac{\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Z}}}{\sqrt{ \paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{X}}\paren{\bar{Z}\cdot\bar{Z}}}}$ $\wh{I}(X;Z) \gets -\frac{1}{2}\log\paren{1 - {\wh{\rho}_{X,Z}}^2}$ \If{$\widehat{I}(X,Z) \geq \eps/8$}{ Output $I(X;Z) \geq \eps$. \ } \Else{ Output $I(X;Z)=0$. \ } \Comment{Conditional MI tester } $\wh{\alpha} \gets \frac{\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Z}}}{\paren{\bar{Z}\cdot\bar{Z}}}$, $\wh\beta \gets \frac{\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Y}}\paren{\bar{Z}\cdot\bar{Z}} - \paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Z}}\paren{\bar{Y}\cdot\bar{Z}}}{\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{X}}\paren{\bar{Z}\cdot\bar{Z}} - \paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Z}}^2}$ $\wh\gamma \gets\frac{\paren{\bar{Y}\cdot\bar{Z}}\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{X}} - \paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Z}}\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Y}}}{\paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{X}}\paren{\bar{Z}\cdot\bar{Z}} - \paren{\bar{X}\cdot\bar{Z}}^2}$ $\widetilde{X}\gets \bar{X}-\widehat{\alpha} \bar{Z}$, $\widetilde{Y}\gets \bar{Y}-(\widehat{\alpha} \widehat{\beta} + \widehat{\gamma}) \bar{Z}$ $\wh{\rho}_{\wt{X},\wt{Y}} \gets \frac{ \paren{\wt{X}\cdot\wt{Y}} }{ \sqrt{ \paren{\wt{X}\cdot\wt{X}} \paren{\wt{Y}\cdot\wt{Y}} } }$ $\wh{I}(X;Y \mid Z) \gets -\frac{1}{2}\log\paren{1 - \wh{\rho}^2_{\wt{X},\wt{Y}}}$ \If{$\wh{I}(X;Y \mid Z) \geq \eps/8$}{ Output $I(X;Y|Z) \geq \eps$. \ } \Else{ Output $I(X;Y|Z)=0$. \ }

elements.paragraph-2411-007: Chain rule of empirical conditional mutual information
elements.sent-2411-231: Now we discuss another crucial ingredient of our technical results: the chain rule of empirical conditional mutual information.
elements.sent-2411-232: Given three random variables $X,Y$, and $Z$, it is a very well-known and useful fact that \begin{equation}I(X;Y) + I(X;Z \mid Y) = I(X;Z) + I(X;Y \mid Z).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-233: However, as we will be estimating the (conditional) mutual information $\widehat{I (X;Y), \widehat{I (X;Z)$, as well as $\widehat{I (X;Y \mid Z)$ and $\widehat{I (X;Z \mid Y)$ using regression, it is not immediately clear if the chain rule continues to hold for the estimated mutual and conditional mutual information between $X,Y$ and $Z$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: We show that the chain rule remains true in this scenario, that is: \begin{equation}\wh{I}(X;Z)+\wh{I}(X;Y \mid Z) =\wh{I}(X;Y)+\wh{I}(X;Z \mid Y)\end{equation} Specifically, we show that our MI and CMI estimators can be seen as that of a linear Gaussian model whose coefficients are such that its covariance matrix coincides with the empirical covariance matrix of the samples.
elements.sent-2411-235: Therefore, the empirical chain rule immediately follows from the chain rule of the aforesaid linear model.
elements.sent-2411-236: We will crucially use this result for structure learning in the realizable setting (\Cref{theo:treelearningrealizableintro ).}

elements.subsection-2411-011: Lower bound of mutual information tester
elements.sent-2411-388: In this subsection, we will prove that our mutual information tester from \Cref{sec:miub is tight, in the sense that, $\Omega(1/\eps)$ samples are necessary to distinguish between the cases $I(X;Y)=0$ and $I(X;Y) \geq \eps$.}
elements.sent-2411-389: The formal statement of our result is as follows: \mitestlb* Before directly proceeding to the proof of the above theorem, we will first prove that a similar lower bound also holds for distinguishing between the following two linear models.
elements.sent-2411-390: The null hypothesis $H_0$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}X_0 \gets U_0 Y_0 \gets V_0\end{equation} The alternate hypothesis $H_1$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}X_1 \gets U_1 Y_1 \gets \sqrt{\eps} \cdot X_1 + V_1\end{equation} The covariance matrix of $H_0$ is the identity matrix: \begin{equation}\Sigma_0 =  1 0 0 1 \end{equation} Similarly, the covariance matrix of $H_1$ is the following: \begin{equation}\Sigma_1 =  1 \sqrt{\eps} \sqrt{\eps} 1+ \eps \end{equation} Thus, $\det(\Sigma_0)=\det(\Sigma_1)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-391: From \Cref{obs:klhellingerbounds , we know that the $\kl$ between $H_0$ and $H_1$ can be expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:klmilb} \kl(H_0 || H_1) = \frac{1}{2} \left(\tr(\Sigma_1^{-1} \Sigma_0) - 2 + \ln\paren{\frac{\det(\Sigma_1)}{\det(\Sigma_0)}}\right)\end{equation} Putting the values of $\Sigma_0$ and $\Sigma_1$ in \Cref{eqn:klmilb , we can say that $\kl(H_0||H_1) = \eps/2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-392: In a similar manner, we can also show that $\kl(H_1||H_0) = \eps/2$.

elements.sent-2411-568: In this experiment, we investigate the relationship between the number of samples required and the desired level of tolerance ($\eps$) for the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to converge to zero.
elements.sent-2411-569: Specifically, we aim to determine the minimum number of samples necessary ($m^*$) for the KL divergence between the original structure distribution and the predicted tree distribution to approach zero within a fixed tolerance $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-570: Let us start with the realizable setting.
elements.sent-2411-571: Let $U_1, V_1, W_1 \sim N(0,1)$ be three iid bits.
elements.sent-2411-572: Then, the realizable tree $R_1$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}Y_1 \gets U_1 Z_1 \gets 0.5Y_1 + W_1 X_1 \gets \sqrt{\eps} Z_1 + V_1\end{equation} For the fixing of tolerance ($\eps$), we are looking at the minimum number of samples ($m$) at which the KL-Divergence between the original tree and the predicted tree using Chow-Liu algorithm is less than or equal to $\frac{\eps {4}$ with probability $\geq 0.95$.}
elements.formula-2411-126: \begin{equation}Y_1 \gets U_1 Z_1 \gets 0.5Y_1 + W_1 X_1 \gets \sqrt{\eps} Z_1 + V_1\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-574: Then, note the least value of $m$ (i.e. $m^*$) for which the KL between predicted tree distribution and original tree distribution is less than or equal to $\frac{\eps {4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-575: The relation between $m^*$ and $\eps$ for the realizable case with tree structure $R_1$ is presented in \Cref{fig:prob1_6_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-576: Here, $m^*$ is approximately inversely proportional to $\eps$.
elements.sent-2411-577: After fitting the linear regression to the $\log(m^*)$ vs $\log\left(\frac{1 {\eps}\right)$ data, we obtain a line with a slope of $1.016$.}

elements.sent-2411-583: In this subsection, we compare the results of our Gaussian Chow-Liu algorithm with Graphical Lasso (GLASSO), a popular algorithm used in the literature for estimation of covariance matrices.
elements.sent-2411-584: Let us start by briefly describing GLASSO.
elements.sent-2411-585: The Graphical Lasso (GLASSO) is an algorithm for estimating sparse inverse covariance matrices in Gaussian graphical models, especially useful for high-dimensional data where the number of variables \( p \) exceeds the number of observations \( n \).
elements.sent-2411-586: It estimates the precision matrix \( \Theta = \Sigma^{-1 \), revealing conditional dependencies between variables.}
elements.sent-2411-587: The precision matrix \( \Theta \) is estimated by solving the optimization problem stated in \cite{friedman2008sparse : \begin{equation}\hat{\Theta} = \underset{\Theta \succ 0}{argmin} \left\{ tr(S \Theta) - \log \det(\Theta) + \lambda \|\Theta\|_1 \right\}\end{equation} Here, \( S \) is the sample covariance matrix, \( \|\Theta\|_1 \) is the element-wise \( \ell_1 \)-norm of \( \Theta \), and \( \lambda \) is a regularization parameter controlling sparsity.}
elements.sent-2411-589: Firstly, we will set the original tree construction similar to \Cref{sec:experiment:chowliu .}
elements.sent-2411-590: Let $U, V, W \sim N(0,1)$ be three iid bits.
elements.sent-2411-591: Then, the realizable tree $R_1$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}Y \gets U Z \gets 0.5Y + W X \gets \sqrt{\eps} Z + V\end{equation} Then, do the following experiment on it.
elements.formula-2411-129: \begin{equation}Y \gets U Z \gets 0.5Y + W X \gets \sqrt{\eps} Z + V\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-592: We used the GLASSO package from R for the experiments~\cite{glassopackage .}
elements.sent-2411-593: GLASSO returns a precision matrix for the triangle graph with the nodes $X,Y,Z$.
elements.sent-2411-594: We simply remove the lowest entry of the returned precision matrix in absolute value to obtain the approximate tree structure and compare the latter with the Chow-Liu output.
elements.sent-2411-595: We conduct 200 trials to ascertain the frequency of correct recovery for various combinations of fixed epsilon and sample size ($m$).
elements.sent-2411-596: This gives us a way to compare the performance of the two algorithms to determine which requires fewer samples to prefer the $X$-$Z$ edge over the $X$-$Y$ edge.
elements.sent-2411-597: We report this frequency of correct recovery in \Cref{fig:glasso_r_1 --\Cref{fig:glasso_r_4 by the above two algorithms for 100 different fixings of samples for $4$ different values of $\eps: 0.1,0.01,0.001,0.0001$.}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{glasso_r_1.png} \caption{Chow-Liu vs GLASSO for $\eps = 0.1$} \label{fig:glasso_r_1} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{glasso_r_2.png} \caption{Chow-Liu vs GLASSO for $\eps = 0.01$} \label{fig:glasso_r_2} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{glasso_r_3.png} \caption{Chow-Liu vs GLASSO for $\eps = 0.001$} \label{fig:glasso_r_3} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.45\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{glasso_r_4.png} \caption{Chow-Liu vs GLASSO for $\eps = 0.0001$} \label{fig:glasso_r_4} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-598: Overall, we can conclude that, for the tree-structured distributions, the Chow-Liu algorithm performs better than the GLASSO algorithm in reconstructing the structure from the samples.
elements.sent-2411-599: In this subsection, we compare the results of our Gaussian Chow-Liu algorithm with CLIME, another popular algorithm used in the literature for estimation of covariance matrices.
elements.sent-2411-600: We start by briefly describing the method.
elements.sent-2411-601: Constrained $\ell_1$ Inverse Covariance Estimation (CLIME) is an algorithm for estimating inverse covariance matrices in Gaussian Graphical models.
elements.sent-2411-602: Similar to GLASSO, this is useful for high-dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-603: The precision matrix \( \Theta \) is estimated by solving the optimization problem stated in \cite{cai2011constrained . \begin{equation}\min_{\Theta} ||\Theta||_1 \ s.t. \ ||\hat{\Sigma} \Theta - I||_{\infty} \leq \lambda\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-604: We will set the original tree construction similar to \Cref{sec:experiment:chowliu , as done for experiments with GLASSO.}
elements.sent-2411-605: Then, the realizable tree $R_1$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}Y \gets U Z \gets 0.5Y + W X \gets \sqrt{\eps} Z + V\end{equation} Then, we perform the following experiment on it.
elements.sent-2411-606: We use the CLIME package from R for the experiments~\cite{climepackage .}
elements.sent-2411-607: CLIME returns a precision matrix for the triangle graph with the nodes $X,Y,Z$.
elements.sent-2411-608: We report this frequency of correct recovery in \Cref{fig:clime_r_1 --\Cref{fig:clime_r_4 by the above two algorithms for 100 different fixings of samples for $4$ different values of $\eps: 0.1,0.01,0.001,0.0001$.}}
elements.sent-2411-609: Similar to the case of GLASSO, we conclude that for the tree-structured distributions, the Chow-Liu algorithm performs better than the CLIME algorithm in reconstructing the structure from the samples.
